transcript.transcript: It is May 23rd. This is Dr. Fiona Vernal and I am in Windsor conducting an oral history with Miss Dawn Jenkins. Thank you so much for agreeing to conduct this interview. If you could say your name and give me verbal consent for doing this interview. My name is Dawn Marie Jenkins, and I give you verbal consent to do this interview. Alright, so let's start start right at the beginning as far back as you can go. Can you tell me as much about your maternal side of the family? Okay. And then we'll do your paternal side. Okay, well, my grandfather came from Manchester, Jamaica as a farm worker. And I want to say he came somewhere around the 40s to Windsor to the farm and he came for like three seasons and his third season he decided he wasn't to return. He had saved enough money for his family back home his baby mother and the kids to build his farm. But when he returned home, his baby mother, she was pregnant and she had another man. So he came back to the-- Let's start over because I'm not sure I'm going to be able to get the phone out. So, yeah, just let's do a second take. Okay. So all the way from the beginning? Yeah. Okay, so my grandfather came here in around the 1940s, farm working in Windsor at the tobacco fields, and he had came three seasons. And his third season of coming, he returned home to not come back to the States because he felt he had made enough money for his family to marry his baby mother and build their farm. But when he returned, she had moved on. So he said, forget this and he came back to the States the next season and wind up that year. My grandmother was from Mississippi and she had kind of bad history there. Her first husband, he killed their baby. And so her looking for companionship her and her sister decided to go to the tobacco field to see if they can meet them some buddy, and so they went [Phone Interruption]. So they went to the farm and they met my grandfather, although my grandfather, he really liked her sister. But my grandmother and her sister, they were from the south. High yellow. My grandfather was more Black, dark skin. So my aunt said, 'no, she wasn't attracted to him.' So my grandmother said, 'well, I'll take him.' [Laughter] All parties agreed to this version of the story? [Laugh] Yes, that's how it was. So let's get some names. So your grandfather's name from Manchester? My grandfather's name was William. I don't know how you say it: Theopotist? Theophilus. Yes. Those Jamaican names. [Laughs] Edwards. I never could say it right. Oh, no we won't have a problem finding him. [Laughs] William Theophilus Edwards. And your grandmother? And my grandmother's name was Rosalie. Her maiden name Shannon. Shannon was the surname? Yeah. And your aunt's name? Her name was... Sylvia. Sylvia Shannon? Yes. So how did they know about, they're in Mississippi, how did they know about tobacco in Connecticut? No. Well, they came up here, okay. So they wind up up here because they didn't come to pick tobacco, but they had a brother that was here and it was like, he told my grandmother, she was like, one of the first ones to come after her brother come here for a better life. There's more opportunities. So my grandmother came up here and she actually scrapped metal. And she would clean, people houses like white people houses in Avon and Simsbury and what like her pay was whatever she found. You know what I'm saying? And back then the price of copper and different things, we still scrap to this day because when I have a truck available I scrap. I understand the words but I don't actually know what it means to scrap. Can you tell me what that means? Scrap metal is like it's recycling. So like how we bring soda bottles and cans back to the Redemption Center. It's the same thing as like when we have functions at the church or the West Indian club, and those big silver pins that we just throw in the trash. They recycle that. And it turns back into something they can use again. And so, scrapping is still going on today like you see, sometimes you might see people riding down the street, they got a stove or something on the side of the road, people pick it up and they bring it to the recycling center, the scrap yard. And then the scrap yard pays you for the weight of whatever product is. Oh, by weight. By weight. But where did your grandmother learn to do that? It was just a thing they did back then. It's survival. We I think from growing up with like West Indian Heritage and Black American heritage, we just learn how to survive, because it wasn't always given to us. And then, when we got something, we just learned to hold on to it because it was more harder for us to obtain it. Then for them. Alright. So back to this love. So this is still in the 1940s? Yes 1940s. Like we like we we looking at I want to say about like maybe '45 about '45. I haven't found a marriage certificate but I probably, I know, I could go to City Hall and get one. Maybe I guess they were doing marriage certificates back then. But they actually got married, I don't know if you're familiar of Cottage Grove Road where the doctors offices are and they have like a place that sells ice cream and they got the little farm animals. That was the justice of the peace. [Laughs] Okay. And that's where they got married. And down the street a little farther when you're heading back to Blue Hills, it's a street called Hillcrest. My grandmother actually, she had, before she met my grandfather, she bought a house back there. It was a one room, it was actually a shack, a one-room shack with land. And the street was called Craw Street and you had to cross a creek to get to her little Shack. So she built a road, which is still a dirt road. And she dug a well for water and then she dug a basement. After that, that's when around the time when she met my grandfather and then he happened to be, and they got married, he was a carpenter by trade. And he worked at this company called Stanley Construction Company and he built like, helped build, like the boat building the gold building downtown. I know like part of Pratt & Whitney. Tons of buildings. I can't tell you everywhere, but those are some of the places that I've like, heard from my grandparents talking, my parents talking, because growing up I always liked to sit in a room with the elders and listen and-- Eaves drop a little? Eaves drop a little. Okay, so do you know how your grandmother even ended up with that little plot of land and the shack on Hillcrest? Well, she just like I said, she wind up working scrapping metal and stuff. And she wind up, she purchased it for $100. $100? [Laughs] And my grandfather, like I said he was a carpenter by trade so he went on different directions with the house. [Laughs] What does that mean? Like he added on to the house so where it was like this one little room he went that way made a long kitchen. Back in the days, I don't know why they did that, but like with older houses, I don't know if you ever been in an older house, but like some of the rooms are joint like the bedrooms like you go through one bedroom to get to the next bedroom and so it's like that. And then he made a part on top the house. So it's a upstairs and then there's bedrooms and the bedrooms got like closets. Did not like walk-in closets, but we used to hide in them. [Laughter] And it had, they have dressers in a wall that he built. And going back a little, my grandmother when she had came here and started her business scrapping, she had met like a homeless man. And she took him in and he worked for her and we grow to know him as our great-grandfather and his name was Charlie Lyber and he died at 103. And that was, I know him to be my great grandfather and my grandmother told him, as long as she had life, he will always have a place to stay. So my grandfather had built him a little apartment over the garage like a one, efficiency apartment. But that was an act of kindness. Was he also from Mississippi? No, he was actually from, we never really know where he was from but I want to say like the Virgin Islands or so he was from some island or something but never really knew. Never really knew if he even even had any kids or family, but he was just, my grandmother just picked him up and cleaned him up. He was an older man and she took him as her father and just as long as I live, or if I ever have kids, you'll always have a place and he always did. She found him in Hartford? Yes, in Hartford. And he never recounted any story about himself? I never heard any stories. Maybe my mom did, but it never got passed down to me. Wow, that's really amazing. So your grandmother sort of like cobbling her family together. And so they eventually like as they built their house up they tried to have children and every time she would get pregnant, she will wind up losing the baby and finally, when she got pregnant with my mom, they made her stop like her work and she was on bed rest, so she had my mom. And although like my grandfather didn't return to Jamaica, he will go back every year. He never forgot about his family, he had four children and they each came as they wanted to come, he would send barrels home and he would go home every year. And then when my mom was born she got to be about five years old and she started like, how little kids be like 'Oh, I don't have nobody play with I need, like I want a brother and sister. I want a brother and sister,' Because her best friend, which is my godmother, I don't know if you know, the Weaverbase, the Weaver-Beys, they're a big family in the Hartford area. Well, the Weaver-Beys, they lived up the street and they had like 13 kids. So my moms, like, 'why we don't have any kids.' And so my grandparents, they started taking in foster children and they wind up having, it was Jimmy, Aaron, Mary, Wanda, Shirley, Nanette, and Mary. Say that again? Aaron, Jimmy, Shirley Wanda, Nanette, Mary. Who else am I missing. That's six. Wait, Jimmy Aaron. Shirley, Annette, Wanda and Mary. Yes, six. And so they raised them. So no, going back a little bit. My grandparents, I'm not sure what year, but they wind up buying a second property in Hartford number four Brook Street or five Brook Street. Five Brook Street, I think it was. And it was a three family building. so as his kids came, and they got married. That was their start up house. Everybody started up on the floor in the house until, an apartment, till they was ready to buy their own home. So first, it was like my uncle Artnell. Art? Artnell. Jamaican name. [Laughs] Yep. Herman. And then my uncle, I mean my aunt, I'm sorry, Hermina and her husband they came. She was already married in Jamaica? Yeah. And then so in nineteen well, I know that one, 1969 my mom got married to my father. June 28th. July, my grandfather went down to marry my aunt, Hermina because my mom sent her wedding dress to her, they were the same size. And they did a wedding there. Wait, he officiated? No, he didn't officiate it, but he just went to in marry her off, being part of the wedding. My mom said her dress cuz it was a beautiful dress and you only can wear it once so she's like, 'yeah, give it to my sister.' And so my grandfather went, they got married and then they came. And then my grandparents helped put them through school. They also lived on Brook Street? They lived on Brook Street. They put them through college, where all had like, really pretty good careers. My uncle Herman, he died when I was a baby, he had a massive heart attack. My uncle Artnell. Now he was the head plumber at Star Hardware in Harford. My aunt Hermina never really knew what she did but her husband, they retired and moved back to Jamaica because he took a position at the University of West Indies. Was president. And he's not no longer president but he's still working there in some kind of capacity. My uncle Kenneth, the last son, he came in 1985. My grandmother died in 1984 and she had a long drawn-out illness. She had suffered a major stroke and when she passed away, we all just cared for her because my family, we don't believe in nursing homes so it's like the family come together. Where my father would go get out of work from Pratt & Whitney and go bath his mother-in-law. Help my grandfather and stuff and that's just how we did things and she wind up dying like a week before Christmas of '84. So in the summer 85 my mom said 'you know, we did a lot of work we need a break, we need a vacation.' And she said, 'I've never been to Jamaica and I want to see where my father's from.' And so we all went me, my grandfather, and my brother didn't want to go, my father, the four of us went and when we went my uncle, he met us at the airport and back then they had they only had these Yugos, these little tiny cars. That was the only thing available to rent. So they rented two of them and me, and my parents drove on one of my grandfather, my uncle drove in the other and we stayed in Mandeville. And, my mother, she said to him like that 'she fell in love with her brother because it was like looking at him was like seeing her father.' He was the spitting image of him and his kindness and everything and so she said, 'when are you coming?' And he said his his papers were ready but he was waiting for his brother Artnell to send him a plane ticket and she said 'well if you know Artnell you might be waiting a long time.' So she said 'look if I can get you on the plane with me going back, are you going to come?' He said 'yeah,' so we got him on a plane. He came back with us. This is Kenneth? Kenneth. And when they got back she told him what she expected of him. He didn't follow through with everything that he was-- How old was Kenneth? In his 20s? No. He was much older. He was much older like probably like 50. He had to be like 50 or maybe late forties, but he was much older. So then the other ones coming younger out of high school, college. So he when he got back here and my parents really like influential in church. He was a custodian at Union Baptist, where you came, and he worked there. And then my grandfather being like, I want to say like him and my grandmother were like probably one of the first black families in Bloomfield because it was a Jewish Community. And that's when cow farms was up where-- What was up? Cow farms. Cow farms. [Laughs] Yeah, it was the hair store on Blue Hills right across from the Mobile Station, that was called called Cow Farms. And then they had at COPACO Center, it was like live animals where they would killed them and they didn't have that Plaza. It was just a grocery store and it was different. I think they had one big department store back then. So anyway, he was friends with, my grandparents, were friends with the owner of the Mobile Station. So I think that was my first job Marty's Mobil. My grandfather got me a job there and he got my uncle, Kenneth, a job working part-time pumping gas, and stocking up the coolers. So he worked between the church and there. And then one day, my dad's best friend, he worked at Colt Firearms. and he said, 'tell Kenneth to come, put an application, I'll get them in.' He got a job there. This is through your dad's best friend? Yes. And he got a job at Colt Firearms and he wind up sending for his family and they came and then his wife, my parents got them in school and stuff and got them situated. I never forget when my cousin's came. I was like, I was so excited because I've always been on that heavyset side. And my cousins, they was real like thin as a marker, you know? So I couldn't fit in a, like in the 80s when we were only wearing Lee Jeans. It was the style. I couldn't fit in them and they could because they had all the different colors and it was just like so exciting, going to the store to help shop for them and like show my cousin like this is what we're wearing up here and stuff like that. So we got them situated in school. [Phone Call Interruption]

lines.line: # Introduction and Consent
> Dr. Fiona Vernal introduces the interview, which is taking place on May 23rd in Windsor. She asks Ms. Dawn Jenkins for her consent to record the interview, which is duly given.
** introduction, consent, interview, Dr. Fiona Vernal, Dawn Jenkins **
keywords: ** introduction, consent, interview, Dr. Fiona Vernal, Dawn Jenkins **

metadata.title: Introduction and Consent
metadata.synopsis: Dr. Fiona Vernal introduces the interview, which is taking place on May 23rd in Windsor. She asks Ms. Dawn Jenkins for her consent to record the interview, which is duly given.
metadata.keywords: introduction, consent, interview, Dr. Fiona Vernal, Dawn Jenkins

lines.line: # Family History: Maternal Side

> Dawn Jenkins begins to share her family history, focusing on her maternal grandfather from Manchester, Jamaica. He worked as a farm laborer in the 1940s in Windsor, saved money, but faced personal challenges upon returning to Jamaica.
** maternal family, grandfather, Manchester, Jamaica, farm laborer **
keywords: ** maternal family, grandfather, Manchester, Jamaica, farm laborer **

metadata.title: Family History: Maternal Side
metadata.synopsis: Dawn Jenkins begins to share her family history, focusing on her maternal grandfather from Manchester, Jamaica. He worked as a farm laborer in the 1940s in Windsor, saved money, but faced personal challenges upon returning to Jamaica.
metadata.keywords: maternal family, grandfather, Manchester, Jamaica, farm laborer

lines.line: # Family History: Paternal Side and Connections

> Dawn elaborates on her grandmother Rosalie from Mississippi and their migration story. She discusses how Rosalie met her grandfather, and touches on Rosalie's earlier difficulties and eventual marriage.
** Rosalie, Mississippi, migration, marriage, family connections **
keywords: ** Rosalie, Mississippi, migration, marriage, family connections **

metadata.title: Family History: Paternal Side and Connections
metadata.synopsis: Dawn elaborates on her grandmother Rosalie from Mississippi and their migration story. She discusses how Rosalie met her grandfather, and touches on Rosalie's earlier difficulties and eventual marriage.
metadata.keywords: Rosalie, Mississippi, migration, marriage, family connections

lines.line: # Building a Life: Family Endeavors

> Dawn shares insights on her grandmother's resourcefulness in America, including buying land and building a home. The family history of involvement in construction and scrapping is further explained.
** resourcefulness, land purchase, construction, scrapping **

metadata.title: Building a Life: Family Endeavors
metadata.synopsis: Dawn shares insights on her grandmother's resourcefulness in America, including buying land and building a home. The family history of involvement in construction and scrapping is further explained.

lines.line: # Acts of Kindness and Family Growth

> The story of Charlie Lyber, a homeless man taken in by the family, is recounted as an example of their compassion. Dawn also talks about her grandmother’s miscarriages and how her family grew through fostering children.
** Charlie Lyber, kindness, fostering, miscarriages, family growth **
keywords: ** Charlie Lyber, kindness, fostering, miscarriages, family growth **

metadata.title: Acts of Kindness and Family Growth
metadata.synopsis: The story of Charlie Lyber, a homeless man taken in by the family, is recounted as an example of their compassion. Dawn also talks about her grandmother’s miscarriages and how her family grew through fostering children.
metadata.keywords: Charlie Lyber, kindness, fostering, miscarriages, family growth

lines.line: # Fostering Tradition and Education

> Jenkins details the integration of foster children into their family and the importance of education within their household. She points out how her grandparents’ efforts helped their children and foster kids achieve educational success.
** foster children, education, family support, integration **
keywords: ** foster children, education, family support, integration **

metadata.title: Fostering Tradition and Education
metadata.synopsis: Jenkins details the integration of foster children into their family and the importance of education within their household. She points out how her grandparents’ efforts helped their children and foster kids achieve educational success.
metadata.keywords: foster children, education, family support, integration

lines.line: # Reconnecting and Expanding Family Legacy

> The interview turns to the family’s efforts to reconnect with Jamaican roots and integrate Kenneth into their lives in the U.S. A narrative of migration, opportunity, and settling in a new community unfolds.
** reconnecting, Jamaica, migration, Kenneth, opportunity **

metadata.title: Reconnecting and Expanding Family Legacy
metadata.synopsis: The interview turns to the family’s efforts to reconnect with Jamaican roots and integrate Kenneth into their lives in the U.S. A narrative of migration, opportunity, and settling in a new community unfolds.

lines.line: # Community and Cultural Transition
> Details emerge about the family's life in a Jewish community, employment opportunities given through social networks, and cultural adjustments for new family members moving from Jamaica.
** community, cultural transition, employment, Jewish community **
keywords: ** community, cultural transition, employment, Jewish community **

metadata.title: Community and Cultural Transition
metadata.synopsis: Details emerge about the family's life in a Jewish community, employment opportunities given through social networks, and cultural adjustments for new family members moving from Jamaica.
metadata.keywords: community, cultural transition, employment, Jewish community

lines.line: # Final Thoughts and Reflections

> Dawn reflects on the importance of family, community support, cultural integration, and the lessons passed down through generations. The session conveys her gratitude for the opportunities and relationships cultivated by her forebears.
** reflections, family, community, cultural integration, gratitude **
keywords: ** reflections, family, community, cultural integration, gratitude **

story.title: Dawn Jenkins Oral History 1.WAV
story.description: This oral history interview conducted by Dr. Fiona Vernal with Miss Dawn Jenkins covers the familial experiences of Jenkins' family, highlighting migration patterns and the struggles and resilience of Jamaican immigrants in the mid-20th century. The narrative details the journey of Jenkins' maternal grandparents from Jamaica and Mississippi to Windsor, focusing on their work in tobacco fields and resilience in establishing a life in the U.S. The story touches on scrapping metal, expanding a small home, and acts of familial kindness. Additionally, it captures the family's involvement in fostering children, securing jobs, buying property, and the cultural integration within the Hartford community.
